title: Fundamentals of Jain Philosophy
introduction: Jain philosophy is best explained through the Nine Tattvas (fundamentals). These principles describe the nature of the soul, karma, and the path to liberation.

nav_tattvas.name: Jiva
nav_tattvas.description: Conscious entity with infinite knowledge and perception potential. The soul is eternal and indestructible but is currently obscured by karma.

nav_tattvas.name: Ajiva
nav_tattvas.description: Everything without consciousness. Includes five types: Dharmastikaya (motion), Adharmastikaya (rest), Akashastikaya (space), Pudgalastikaya (matter), and Kala (time).

nav_tattvas.name: Punya
nav_tattvas.description: Results of wholesome actions (charity, helping others, religious activities) that bring happiness and comfort.

nav_tattvas.name: Pap
nav_tattvas.description: Results of harmful actions (violence, anger, greed) that bring suffering and misery.

nav_tattvas.name: Asrava
nav_tattvas.description: The inflow of karmic particles into the soul due to wrong belief, passions, negligence, or activities.

nav_tattvas.name: Bandh
nav_tattvas.description: The actual binding of karma particles to the soul caused by attachment and aversion.

nav_tattvas.name: Samvar
nav_tattvas.description: Preventing new karma from entering the soul through carefulness, self-control, and right conduct.

nav_tattvas.name: Nirjara
nav_tattvas.description: The process of removing accumulated karma — either passively (Akam Nirjara) or actively through penance and meditation (Sakam Nirjara).

nav_tattvas.name: Moksha
nav_tattvas.description: Complete freedom from all karma, leading to eternal bliss and infinite knowledge.

analogy.story: A family in a farmhouse during a dust storm
analogy.mapping: The house represents the soul, dust represents karma, closing windows = Samvar, cleaning = Nirjara, clean house = Moksha.

SUMMARY:
This article introduces the Nine Fundamentals (Nav Tattvas) of Jainism. These principles explain 
the nature of the soul (Jiva), non-living matter (Ajiva), the mechanism of karma (Punya, Pap, Asrava, Bandh), and 
the path to liberation through stopping new karma (Samvar), removing old karma (Nirjara), and ultimately attaining Moksha. 
The page uses a simple farmhouse dust storm analogy to make these abstract concepts easy to understand.
